title: Today's AI Brief: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab
summary: A look at AI prototypes from the Futures Lab and an AI startup cleaning homes for free
imageAlt: Image from source article for Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageCaption: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageSourceTitle: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.

body.text: Welcome to Today's AI Brief. I'm Leo Stone, and I'm excited to share some fresh developments in the world of artificial intelligence. We're going to explore some real-life AI prototypes and a startup that's taking a unique approach to training future robots.

First up, the Google-funded Futures Lab at the University of Waterloo is making waves with AI prototypes that are changing the way we learn. Imagine being able to learn Japanese through custom AI-generated stories or practice sign language with an AI tutor that gives real-time feedback. These aren't just ideas - they're working prototypes built by students. It's amazing to see how AI can make complex skills more accessible.

Next, an AI startup called Shift is taking a... let's say, 'unconventional' approach to training future robots. They're offering to clean your home for free in exchange for allowing their 'magic hat' to record their cleaners at work. The goal is to gather data to train future robots. It's a bit like having a robot intern, but with cleaning - and a free cleaning service, no less!

Another aspect of AI development is the economics of model training. With AI requiring massive amounts of data, startups like Shift are finding creative ways to gather it. This raises interesting questions about data boundaries and the future of AI adoption. As AI becomes more prevalent, we're seeing new business models emerge that we didn't see before.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
